text: This review examines recent and noteworthy developments in the chemical preparation of chiral nanographenes, a compelling class of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) that combine the structural elegance of inherent chirality with the significant optical, electronic, and magnetic potential of graphitic units. While nanographene synthesis has traditionally relied heavily on the Scholl oxidation, the ubiquity of that method in recent literature warrants a dedicated look at alternative strategies. Consequently, this work focuses primarily on chiral architectures—including twistacenes, helicenes, imide-functionalized systems, and irregular non-alternant frameworks—prepared via non-Scholl methods such as alkyne-based benzannulations, metal-catalyzed cycloadditions, and radical cascades. By highlighting these unique and often under-reviewed synthetic pathways, we aim to illustrate how rational design can be used to fine-tune the chiroptical and electronic properties essential for the next generation of nanoelectronic applications.
